item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, that's not what would happen. Antimatter interacts with itself just like matter interacts with itself. Antimatter is associated with DESTRUCTIVE POWER because we happen to be made of matter. From antimatter's perspective, we're the DESTRUCTIVE POWER.

However, there would be many problems if something just reversed charge without reversing parity. For one, this would turn all interacting neutrinos into their sterile counterparts and vice versa. This would mean that you could only detect neutrinos if the emitter and detector were an even number of charge-reversals away from each other, timewise. (The Sun would go neutrino-dark for approximately eight minutes after every reversal.) So the antimatter-conversion process wouldn't take complicated probing into kaon decay to notice - neutrino detector sensitive enough to see the Sun would see it.

Fortunately, SCP-2123 doesn't just reverse charge. It also reverses parity. So everything is flipped around a mirror, resulting in left-handed neutrinos becoming right-handed. (Why don't you notice yourself becoming left-handed? Because all our benchmarks for "left" have been flipped as well, even neurological ones.)

item.title: Last Part Seems Contrived
item.content:encoded:
How does a matter/antimatter flip single out one specific person at a time and everything they've come into contact with? Especially database stuff—data are very compressed in modern computers, and I'd imagine the Foundation would have much more advanced or even anomalously infinite storage that takes up tiny amounts of space. Basically, I don't buy that a matter/antimatter flip could wipe out specific data stored on some version of a hard drive without massively impacting anything else. The way the researcher vanished was weird too. Is it implying that he slowly turned to matter/antimatter over the course of an hour? If so, that makes absolutely no sense. Even spread out over an hour, that much antimatter would cause much more damage than mentioned.

Grudging upvote because I like everything before that part, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About 90% of this I'm with you on, even without a quantum physics background I still got the general idea pretty well.

The problem? The last addendum and similar events implied earlier. Utter destruction, yes, of my suspension of disbelief. The matter/antimatter distribution should be roughly uniform across the material plane - you wouldn't expect there to be significant concentrations of it, and thus not points where it would be significantly targeted. That means that it wouldn't be reasonable to expect that matter in the exact form of a contiguous human would be subject to complete annihilation. Nope, that doesn't make any sense at all.

And if THAT doesn't make sense, it makes even LESS sense for this effect to extend beyond the physical presence of the individual to things that they no longer have contiguous contact which that they happened to be generally in the presence of at some point in the past. It is COMPLETELY beyond believable that the doctor's records would be erased simply because he co-existed in the same sphere with them at varying points in the past. There's no basis for this in the matter/antimatter flip idea, and the write-up would be better without it.

Granted, it's personal opinion here - one of my pet peeves when it comes to SCPs is the tacked-on "oh and it randomly kills you" that has little to nothing to do with the primary anomaly. But I think others have expressed similar reservations about those parts, if for different reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no problem. The matter-creation SCPs and the matter-destruction SCPs exactly balance each other out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it sounds more like a 3rd party is assassinating them and erasing whatever they did to remove evidence/research progress on this front, at least to me. Sounds more plausible too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I realize this is an old post, but it's still my biggest sticking point in the article, so I'm going to reply anyway.
Assuming our late doctor weighs about 70 kg, everyone's favorite physics equation gives us a total energy of about 6.3 exajoules, or 3 gigatons of TNT. That's not enough to blow up the Earth, but it's also not something that could possibly be released at the Earth's surface on the timescale of hours, weeks, months, or even years without absurdly terrible consequences. The addendum makes it sound like the doctor's body became like a mildly-critical nuclear core ("minor damage to the medical ward, and 3 medical personnel receiving non-fatal doses") but in reality, assuming typical annihilation models, it would be like the hair dryer box in this wonderful xkcd What If? article, radiating heat in the petawatts or hundreds of terawatts and turning the ground into lava and the air into plasma. (Divide 6.2×10¹⁸ Joules by however many seconds the process takes, and find the corresponding part of that article - you can take out a few tens of percent for optimistic models of neutrino losses, but that's not going to make the scene much less hellish.)

Toss in anomalous disappearances all you like, but by the that point in the article, my physics background was already chewing on my suspension of disbelief with the whole Excursion Event deal, and the final addendum really pushed it over the edge into "not buying it" territory.
I'm not downvoting because the rest of the article before that point was really pretty good, but I'm not upvoting either. I'll post a more holistic comment down-thread sometime later, after I've hashed out my thoughts a little more.

item.title: Frankly, I don't like this.
item.content:encoded:
It's an anomalous particle accelerator that… does physics-y stuff in a way I don't understand (in the banal, "I don't understand this" way, not the scary "what's going on??" way), which ends up with the universe being slowly deleted because it's being converted into anti-matter?
Without any background in physics, this is heavy and confusing with a punchline that comes out of nowhere. I'm sorry, but downvote it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Late reply but I decided to check to math on this.

The observable universe is estimated to contain about 1053kg of matter. A kilogram is 103 grams, so that gives us 1056 grams.

A yottagram, the unit used here is 1024 grams. The order of magnitude listed in the article is 1027 Yg. Convert the units and we get…

1051 grams.

So yeah that actually gives us .00001 times the mass of the universe, or .001%, which is actually even worse than what you said.

Either that was the one thing I forgot to check my numbers on, or I didn't think .001% of the universe going away was a problem. Not sure why I thought that, but whatever. If we assume the radius of effect espands at the speed of light, I don't even think it would have covered enough area at this point to destroy that much matter if it was destroying everything it affected.

I'll change it to about 1Yg, because Yottagram is a cool word and I want to keep it. Thanks for pointing that out, and I can't believe it took 2 years for this to be noticed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I think this thing is a bit too destructive—looking up the total mass of the observable universe, it's estimated to be around 10^53 kilograms (not including dark matter). So according to this, we're already missing around 0.001% of the observable universe, if the first number blacked out is one.

And while I'm not an astrophysicist, it sounds like this would already doom us due to the radiation released by matter-antimatter annihilation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs don't have to be scary. At this point, we're more of a collaborative writing website than a Creepypasta archive, and there are plenty of non-scary SCPs out there (SCP-348, SCP-1370 and SCP-1171 are just a few examples.) In the end, an SCP should be judged for its quality, not how scary it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written, but ultimately will have to downvote. The average person would find this a very heavy read with in my opinion not a lot of payoff at the end. It's interesting to think that this thing creates holes in reality and removes people and their work from existence, but ultimately it isn't very scary, in my opinion, which after all is the main goal of writing SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would say if the world was converted to antimatter everything would have collapsed and we'd already be dead by now - of course this is coming from a person who never looked at physics since 10 years ago.
From the looks of it it may be either that 1. if the universe is converted as a whole it remains relatively stable or 2. only the limited area, so far well contained, is converted.

Whatever the answer is, another question emerges; why did that doctor have to disappear? If 1. (mentioned above) is true then there is no reason he himself as a whole couldn't remain stable. Are you implying this machine is partially sapient and targets whatever/whoever it wants to get rid of?

item.title: Re: @Waterfire re: SCP-2123
item.content:encoded: They were a definitely some sort of research corporation.

item.title: @Waterfire re: SCP-2123
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, but my question was: what exactly is this FHI?
And what about the important words?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He wasn't annihilated all at once. The energy was spread out over time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FHI built the thing (it's explained in the article).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hear, hear! What Rumetzen said!
Far beyond my feeble grasp of particle physics, but convincing, smart, well-written and interesting. It makes me want to trust the author that yes, s/he knows what s/he talking about.
Upvote.

PS what actually is this FHI company?
And I too would love a clarification on this:

High levels of radiation continued to be emitted, before subsiding over the course of the next hour.

Why are the last few words so important?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the point, please spoonfeed me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if I don't understand half of it, I love hard SF stuff like this and it's a niche on the wiki that deserves to be filled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tesla's doomsday device is SCP-2700

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't this used to be an entropy-reversing doomsday device created by Tesla? Or am I thinking of something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Points for using "YK-Class" in regards to universe-affecting scenarios. Still rereading this one so that I can process the more technical aspects, so withholding vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I really don't want to just hand you the explanation. Let me try giving you a hint:

Dr. Leary’s body began to emit high levels of gamma radiation, resulting in minor damage to the medical ward, and 3 medical personnel receiving non-fatal doses. High levels of radiation continued to be emitted, before subsiding over the course of the next hour.

High levels of radiation continued to be emitted, before subsiding over the course of the next hour.

over the course of the next hour.

…OK, I think you get the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I got the physics, and the implications of the anti-matter <-> matter flip are extremely scary. One malfunction in this machine, and the universe annihilates. That part is great.

Then I got to the level-5 addendum. It sucks. There is no reason why the machine, which operates on universal or at least planet-wide scale, should bother erasing one random researcher. Moreover, if it erased that researcher by making them anti-matter and the rest of the universe normal matter, or vice-versa, the planet would be destroyed by the resultant explosion.

So, upvoted for everything but that bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, this gets a no-vote from me. I can easily see that you put a lot of effort into this, but sadly I can't understand half of what it's saying. I know what mesons, antimatter, and such things are, but not in depth enough to actually get what the article was telling me. I also didn't know about the matter/antimatter asymmetry, so I had to take the article's word when it said what was happening is bad.

Not gonna upvote because I didn't enjoy it, but not gonna downvote either because it isn't a bad SCP, you just gotta know your physics to get all the enjoyment out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of this flew right over my head. I think I understood the implications of the thing well enough though. Also, the Dr. Dominick in the first email was missing its period. I feex

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was quite a dense document to read through, and I didn't find the implications presented about the universe changing to be all that interesting. All that seemed to actually happen was a doctor and some of his mates got wiped out. Maybe there's more to the science that I don't understand, or I missed something. Neutral voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's more what I was going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written with excellent use of science. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing, I like it. I have a few problems with the execution, which I detailed to the author in chat, so I won't repeat them here. But overall I think this is a good, upvote-worthy article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My take from this is that he wasn't retroactively removed, but rather everything he directly created was converted out-of-phase just like he was. So, ink in anything he wrote, bytes in any files he created, etc. And then poof, and it's all gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit, I didn't understand what was going on here at all until I read the comments. But considering all the hard work that you've clearly put into this one, I can't help but upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Matter is being converted every two weeks to anti-matter, and back again. Apparently, the Doctor who was 'annihilated' was retroactively removed from existence. But it seems to have been kinda hap-hazard, as memories of him readily exist, and you can refer to him. It's just, everything he ever did personally has been undone on some level.

This is worse than him just being entirely removed retroactively. If he were entirely removed, reality would've changed to fit his absence. But it hasn't. There are gaps forming in reality and paradoxes building up over time because it's happening to others as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say the level 4 addendum just barely explains what's going on, and I actually found the level 5 addendum more interesting overall. I can't figure out what the link is, but in a good, tantalizing way. Also, I'd change "adequate clearance" to "sufficient clearance"; sounds a little better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, if it helps, I know next to jack about particle physics and I was still starting to get it by the time the addendum handed it to me. It helped that I knew about matter/antimatter asymmetry, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't know there was going to be high-energy physics on this exam.

What I'm getting is, either:
(1) this SCP object is causing all matter in the universe to be converted into antimatter, and vice versa, every two weeks or so. And although most of the time nobody notices the change, it's dangerous.

Or,

(2) all matter in the universe is spontaneously converting itself into antimatter, and vice versa, every two weeks or so. And generally speaking, nobody notices. But this anomalous particle accelerator is /creating a localized exception/ to that, and that's dangerous.

Is either of these correct? Which one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
M = |M_{1}|e^{i\theta_{1}}e^{i\phi_{1}} + |M_{2}|e^{i\theta_{2}}e^{i\phi_{2}}\tilde{M} = |M_{1}|e^{i\theta_{1}}e^{-i\phi_{1}} + |M_{2}|e^{i\theta_{2}}e^{-i\phi_{2}}

… would you read it/explain it to me pleeeees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-vote. This article has its heart in the right place, and the commitment to developing a narrative and having a coherent story prevent me from downvoting. I like the idea of the description being somewhat misleading in terms of the true significance of the object, in order to allow the story to unfold in the addenda. And the technical detail is obviously well-researched, to the point where you've convinced me at least that you know what you're talking about. I do think VAE is correct that this is somewhat of a niche piece, and that's good. We should have those.

That all said, I had a hard time with flow. The reader has to do a lot of slogging to get to the good stuff here, something which is tricky to manage, I know, but I do believe in a cohesive document that maintains a reader's interest throughout. There is some intriguing material once you get through the very very dry front portion, but the taste of the grudging physics lesson (thanks for that phrasing, Dajvj) isn't completely washed away.

Also, I believe that this article is one of the few cases where a researcher directly explaining the implications is warranted. As a layman, I for one have no goddamn clue why switching back and forth from matter to antimatter is bad, other than it sounding cosmically pretty horrible to my science-rube sensibilities in a "they stole all of our internets" kind of way. So yes, it does need to be explained to me. But I feel like since you're there doing that, it kind of needs to go all the way. Why does matter being slightly more stable than anti-matter make this so problematic? I feel like I got kind of an explanation, but I'm still left kind of scratching my head.

Lastly, I feel like you could have dropped just a little more in the way of hints earlier in the article as to why all of a sudden people who are trying to contain this are being radiation-murdered and then corpse-annihilated. I don't object to this as a plot device at all, but it felt kind of sudden, the way it was introduced at the very end like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I barely understand it, but the tone is spot-on throughout, and the science appears to be well-researched. The stuff about the site being deserted and the FHI employees all having disappeared was suitably creepy. +1!

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Glad you like it!

item.title: A few Overlooked Details
item.content:encoded: There are multiple spots where you forgot to replace their SCP Designation placeholder (instead of 2123, it says "y"). However, I like the detail you put in with the collapsibles. Adds to my immersion. +1

650mSv

Thank you for knowing the difference between dose and exposure
Thank you for knowing the difference between dose and exposure
Thank you for knowing the difference between dose and exposure
Thank you for knowing the difference between dose and exposure

[REDACTED, SEE ADDENDUM 2123-ALPHA, LEVEL 4 CLEARANCE REQUIRED]

This should be a semicolon after REDACTED tho

In fact, though that would cut it, I'd change it to

[REDACTED; SEE ADDENDUM 2123-ALPHA (LEVEL 4 CLEARANCE REQUIRED)]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a cool thought, a YK-Scenario happens bi-weekly. But I feel that the last note was kind-of hanging of the edge of "believable" and "un-believable". I may be missing something, since I don't understand a lot of physics. Writing is good, tone, spelling and the rest is fantastic, so is the idea, but the last note felt a bit abrupt.

Sorry, but no-vote. May be liable to change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, like what everyone else has said, I'm just too uninformed about particle physics to understand most of this. I think I still managed to understand this for the most part, tho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Impressive.
Not nearly as good as 1718, partly because I imagine a background in particle physics is what you need for this one to truly work (the gradual realization of the punchline yourself, if you will) but it's still a great article.

I'd say, don't change a thing. This is and will be a niche article, but it's a very good one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, a minor misspelling: it's spelled Waxahachie. You did better than most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted despite begrudgingly reading through a physics lesson. The hook was worth it, and I think I learned something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It looks like you've done your research really well, and I know enough about antimatter for the twist near the end to make sense and be kind of a cool hook, but…most of the text of the article is so in-depth that I just glaze over while reading it. Unfortunately, I can't think of a good way to fix this (and retain the article's integrity.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's an article about the phenomenon on Wikipedia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get it

would you read it/explain it to me ples?

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
After 4 months of work, it's finally done. why the heck did this take this long

Photo is edited from Wikipedia. Thanks to Faminepulse for helping with the ionization effect.
